---
title: "Preface"
description: "Why this book exists, what it covers, and the notation, evidence, and reading conventions it holds to."
page-role: frontmatter
---

# Preface {.unnumbered}

Most people meet electronics twice. The first time it is a set of rules that
happen to work: memorize the formula, recognize the circuit, trust the answer.
The second time—on a bench, at 2 a.m., with a board that refuses to do what the
formula promised—it becomes something else entirely. This book is written for
the second meeting, and it tries to spare you as much of the 2 a.m. confusion as
a book honestly can.

Its central conviction is simple. A working circuit is a single object that we
happen to describe in many languages: the physical assembly and its energy path,
the schematic with its named nodes, the governing equations, a simulation, the
firmware, the measured waveform, the requirement, and the test that accepts it.
When those descriptions agree, you understand the circuit. When they disagree,
one of them is wrong—and the disagreement is the most useful thing the bench
will tell you all day. Everything in this book is organized around producing,
and then reconciling, those descriptions. It follows the bench-first tradition
of practical electronics [@horowitz2015art] while grounding device behavior in
the quantitative circuit models of the standard microelectronics literature
[@sedra2020microelectronic], and it treats the reconciliation itself—not any
single number—as the skill worth learning.

## Who this book is for

It is written for anyone who wants to *understand* electronics well enough to
build something trustworthy, and who is willing to earn that understanding on a
bench rather than accept it on authority. In practice that includes the
self-directed learner starting from a battery and a multimeter, the
undergraduate who wants the through-line their courses assume but rarely state,
the software engineer moving toward hardware, and the working practitioner who
needs a coherent reference across domains they do not touch every day.

The only firm prerequisite is a willingness to predict before you measure and to
believe the measurement afterward. Mathematics is developed just in time, in the
appendices, when a chapter first needs it. The book is, and will remain, free.

## What the book covers

The path is long on purpose: a single measured voltage and a field-qualified
instrument are the same craft practiced at different scales. It runs in seven
parts, each answering one central question and handing a durable capability to
the next.

- **Foundations** ask what voltage, current, resistance, energy, and a
  *trustworthy* measurement actually are. This part establishes the habits—safe
  practice, reference directions, model limits, and uncertainty—that every later
  part silently assumes.
- **Devices and analog** ask how physical devices shape and amplify real
  signals, from the semiconductor junction through transistors, amplifiers,
  feedback, sensors, and the limits imposed by noise and bandwidth
  [@sedra2020microelectronic].
- **Digital hardware** asks how values, decisions, state, and computation are
  built from those same analog circuits, building toward memory, processors, and
  reconfigurable logic [@harris2021digital].
- **Embedded systems** ask how software meets time, energy, peripherals, and
  failure—the place where a correct algorithm still misses a deadline, and a
  clean interrupt still corrupts a shared variable.
- **System domains** ask how we sample, process, control, power, and communicate.
  Sampling and signal processing follow the standard signals-and-systems
  treatment [@oppenheim1996signals]; feedback and control draw on modern
  feedback-systems theory [@astrom2021feedback]; power, storage, RF, and
  connectivity extend the same models outward.
- **Product realization** asks how a design becomes reproducible, compliant, and
  maintainable hardware, taking electromagnetic compatibility as a design
  constraint rather than a final test [@ott2009electromagnetic].
- **Complete systems** ask how the earlier details hold up under load: a
  precision instrument, a resilient data logger, a secure connected node, and a
  context-responsive field system, ending in a capstone that must be qualified,
  handed to another person, and improved.

Alongside these run the appendices (M01–M03, T01–T03): algebra through
differential equations, probability and numerical methods, and the working
practices of reading datasheets and standards, using tools and version control,
and keeping records another person can reproduce. They are references used when
a chapter reaches for them, not a hurdle to clear before starting.

Course routes are published separately and point to these same chapters. There
are no course-specific copies of the theory and no chapter forked for a
particular semester; a concept used by three courses is still written once.

## What a chapter promises

A chapter in this book has three jobs. First, it makes a model intelligible.
Second, it connects that model to observable evidence. Third, it uses the
evidence to make a design or diagnostic decision. A chapter that performs only
the first job—however elegant its derivation—is incomplete, and the book treats
it as such.

That structure shapes the working method the whole text asks of you: **predict,
build, measure, reconcile, decide.** Before a result is revealed, predict its
direction and rough magnitude, and state the model and its limits. Then compare
calculation, simulation, and measurement, and read any disagreement as
information rather than noise. Finish by deciding what the evidence actually
permits you to claim—no stronger.

## Representations kept in agreement

Keeping the descriptions of one system aligned is the recurring discipline of
the book. An important example tries to connect several of these views at once:

- physical construction and the energy path;
- schematic with named nodes and reference directions;
- equations and their assumptions;
- simulation or another executable model;
- measured data with its uncertainty;
- software, state, and timing;
- requirements and the tests that accept them.

Code and simulation expose their parameters rather than hide them in a
screenshot, and raw data stays available beside the analysis that interprets it,
so that any reader can re-run the reconciliation rather than take it on trust.

## Notation and conventions

SI units are used throughout [@bipm2019si]. Voltage polarity and current
reference direction are defined *before* the equations that use them, because a
sign is meaningless until its reference is stated. A negative result therefore
means reality opposes the chosen reference; it is not automatically an error.
The book keeps equality, approximation, tolerance, bound, and uncertainty
verbally and typographically distinct, and it says whether an amplitude is
instantaneous, peak, peak-to-peak, RMS, average, phasor, or spectral density
rather than leaving it to context. Every compact model is named together with
the operating conditions and validity range that make it true.

## Evidence and the weight of a claim

Measurement uncertainty is treated as a first-class quantity to be estimated,
not an afterthought to be waved away [@jcgm2008gum]. Behind every result sits an
evidence ladder that runs from a rough estimate to a qualified claim:

```text
estimate → calculation → simulation → measurement → repeated test
         → independent reproduction → field evidence → qualified claim
```

Not every question needs the top rung. The consequence of being wrong sets how
much evidence is appropriate: a sign convention can rest on a calculation, while
a safety or lifecycle claim cannot. Learning to choose the right rung—and to
stop there—is itself part of the engineering.

## How to use this book

The default route follows the sidebar from elements to systems, and it is the
safest route for a new reader because later parts assume the measurement habits,
reference directions, model limits, and evidence practices established in Part I.
Readers who already know basic circuit theory can use the chapter prerequisites
in [`chapters.toml`](chapters.toml) to enter later without breaking a dependency.
Readers aiming at a specific destination—a bench investigation, an analog
instrument, an embedded system, a custom product, or a complete field system—can
use the [reading roadmap](roadmap.qmd), which also explains chapter maturity and
maps the practice-and-project spine that turns reading into evidence.

Reach for the appendices just in time: follow a prerequisite link when a chapter
first needs the technique, rather than reading them front to back.

To build a lecture from the book, select a small number of chapter sections,
surface one misconception, work one example, and create one decision or exit
check. Slides provide cues; the book provides the durable exposition. The
[instructor guide](../instructor/README.md) develops this into templates.

## Maturity, and an honest boundary

This is a development edition. The table of contents and the dependency
structure are complete, but the chapters are currently outlines: each reports
its own maturity in its front matter, and an outline is a scope commitment, not
yet a teachable or safety-authorizing chapter. The book would rather tell you
what it does not yet contain than imply a completeness it has not earned.

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary"}
Early practical work is deliberately low-voltage and current-limited. Reading a
chapter is not authorization for mains, high-voltage, high-energy battery, RF
power, rotating-machine, medical, or safety-critical work. Use approved
facilities, supervision, and local procedures.
:::

## Sources, and how they are used

Where a claim rests on device physics, metrology, or regulation, the book points
to primary and canonical sources rather than restating them from memory. The
works cited here—practical electronics [@horowitz2015art], microelectronic
circuits [@sedra2020microelectronic], signals and systems [@oppenheim1996signals],
digital design and computer architecture [@harris2021digital], feedback systems
[@astrom2021feedback], electromagnetic compatibility [@ott2009electromagnetic],
the SI [@bipm2019si], and the guide to expressing measurement uncertainty
[@jcgm2008gum]—are the reference spine the chapters build against, and the full
[bibliography](references.qmd) grows with the technical content it supports.

None of them is a substitute for a datasheet, a standard, or supervised
laboratory training, and neither is this book. Its aim is narrower and, we hope,
more durable: to leave you able to say which model you are using, what would
break it, and exactly how much your evidence lets you claim. When you can do
that with a single voltage, you can do it with a system.

Begin with [safe practice](01-foundations/f01-safe-practice.qmd).
